content.text: Once upon a time, a lonely woman who wished for a child visited a wise witch. The witch gave her a magical barleycorn, which the woman planted in a flowerpot.
Soon, a magnificent tulip-like flower bloomed. When the woman gently kissed its petals, they burst open, revealing a tiny, exquisitely beautiful girl sitting upon the green pistil.
She was no larger than a human thumb, and so her mother named her Thumbelina. A varnished walnut shell served as her cradle, blue violet petals were her mattress, and a rose leaf was
her blanket. By day, she sang in a voice sweeter than any bell, floating on a tulip petal across a plate of water. One night, a hideous old toad crept through the open window.
Seeing the sleeping girl, the toad decided Thumbelina would make a perfect wife for her ugly son. She snatched the walnut shell and carried it to the brook, placing Thumbelina on
a tiny water-lily leaf. When Thumbelina awoke, she wept bitterly at the prospect of living in the muddy swamp.
Hearing her cries, the kind little fish of the brook nibbled through the lily pad’s stem, allowing the leaf to float away down the river.
A beautiful white butterfly guided her journey, but suddenly, a great cockchafer beetle swooped down, seized her in his claws, and flew up into a tree.
However, the other beetles mocked her for only having two legs and no feelers, so the cockchafer set her down on a daisy, leaving her alone.
Thumbelina spent the summer and autumn in the great forest, living on honey from the flowers and dew from the leaves. But when winter arrived, the cold became unbearable.
Shivering and wrapped in a dry leaf, she stumbled upon the warm dwelling of a field mouse. The kind, elderly mouse took pity on the frozen girl, offering her shelter in exchange for
keeping the house clean and telling stories. Soon, their wealthy neighbor, a blind mole in a velvet coat, came to visit.
He fell in love with Thumbelina’s sweet voice and began planning to marry her, which filled her with dread, as she hated the dark, subterranean life he lived.
While exploring the mole’s long, dark tunnels, Thumbelina discovered a frozen swallow that had collapsed from the cold.
Filled with compassion, she secretly nursed the bird throughout the winter, weaving a warm blanket of hay and bringing him food.
When spring arrived, the swallow recovered, thanked his tiny savior, and flew out into the warm sunshine. Summer passed, and Thumbelina’s wedding to the mole drew near.
Desperate to escape a life forever hidden from the sun, she went outside to say her final farewell to the beautiful sky. Suddenly, a familiar chirping filled the air.
It was the grateful swallow! Hearing of her plight, the bird urged her to climb onto his back. Thumbelina joyfully agreed, tying herself to his strongest feather.
They flew over towering mountains and sparkling seas until they reached a warm, sunny land filled with fragrant blossoms.
The swallow gently placed her inside a brilliant white flower, where she was amazed to find a tiny, handsome youth with a golden crown and delicate wings.
He was the King of the Flower Angels. He fell instantly in love with her gentle spirit and offered her his crown.
Thumbelina happily accepted, received her own pair of wings, and was renamed Maia, living in eternal sunshine and joy among the flowers.
